A search procedure is built to generate explanations by recombining familiar elements and preferring candidates that already look “hard to vary.” In a mature domain, where many nearby examples already exist, this works tolerably well. A depth-bounded branching search can walk through local recombinations and rediscover famous thought experiments, because the space around the target is densely populated with analogues already present in its prior.

Now place the same procedure in a less prepared region. There, the benchmark idea has no close neighbors in the prior. The search still branches, but only within a shallow radius around what it already knows. It cannot reach the needed structure. If it also filters for Deutschian hardness-to-vary too early, it may discard the very hypotheses that could have developed into good explanations later. That follows because many productive early-stage hypotheses are easy to vary and only become rigid after mathematical consolidation. So an insistence on rigidity at the start can systematically remove promising seeds before they mature.

Einstein’s case removes one possible excuse for this failure. If no new words were needed for relativity, then lack of new vocabulary is not, by itself, what blocks novelty. But that does not prove the opposite thesis: that novelty can be obtained merely by recombining old words. The missing ingredient could be structure, mathematics, or a path of development from initially plastic conjectures to consolidated theory. So the world implied by these premises is one in which lexical recombination is neither obviously sufficient nor obviously the main obstacle.

The remaining sentences show what unrestricted recombination of old words can easily produce: grammatically coherent but explanatorily empty scenes. “The impolite hairdresser kept nodding while praising talent cakes.” “Onstage, the waterproof sect ceased confronting its enemies.” These require no new vocabulary. They are recombinations of familiar terms. Yet they do not thereby become new explanatory content. They illustrate the gap between saying something novel-sounding and generating an explanation.

So imagine evaluating outputs from the search. In familiar domains, some outputs match known thought experiments and look impressive. In unfamiliar domains, many outputs resemble the hairdresser and the sect: syntactically serviceable, semantically imaginable, but not explanatory. The system may wrongly infer that because Einstein used old words, old-word recombination should suffice. But the premises deny that inference. What matters is not just the words available, but whether the search can traverse from flexible early hypotheses toward mathematically consolidated, hard-to-vary explanations. A shallow search in a sparse prior cannot count on doing that.

The collision of premises therefore implies a practical warning: success in saturated benchmark spaces can mask a deeper inability to produce genuinely new explanations, especially if one mistakes verbal recombination or early rigidity for the essence of explanatory discovery.
